course.title: Example Algorithms Course
course.description: A minimal but valid sample course package for the public open course repository.

patterns.title: Binary Search Basics
patterns.description: Learn the binary search pattern with a small, importable example.

lessons.title: Binary Search Warmup
lessons.intro: Binary search repeatedly halves a sorted search space. This sample lesson is intentionally small but still satisfies the Mokumoku schema requirements.

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?
questions.explanation: Binary search cuts the remaining search space in half each step, which leads to logarithmic time.
questions.hint: Think about what happens to the remaining search range after each comparison.
questions.concreteExample: A phone book lookup discards half the remaining pages each time you jump to the middle.

whyFollowUp.question: Why does halving the search space produce a logarithmic running time?
whyFollowUp.answer: Because after k steps only n / 2^k candidates remain, so solving n / 2^k = 1 gives k = log2(n).

misconceptions.a: Linear search checks one element at a time. Binary search removes half the candidates with each comparison.
misconceptions.c: O(n log n) is common for sorting, not for searching a sorted array by repeatedly halving the space.
misconceptions.d: You usually need more than one comparison unless the middle element is the target immediately.

questions.prompt: Order the steps for a standard binary search on a sorted array.
questions.explanation: You start with the full range, inspect the midpoint, and discard the half that cannot contain the target.

items.text: Compare the midpoint value with the target
Discard the impossible half of the range

questions.prompt: To avoid overflow, compute the midpoint as left + (right - ______) / 2.
questions.explanation: Using right - left avoids summing two potentially large integers directly.
